auto_merge.commit_message:
... and manage them with dependabot.

I've intentionally left the version out of project.toml and installed it separately
with a file used only on our CI (so as not to put artificial barriers on future users
trying to install it somewhere we hadn't anticipated).

I tried using the argument to `build` `--dependency-constraints-txt`.
That's unfortunately too new so didn't work.

For Cython 3.2.x we'll need a slightly more complicated requirements file
with different versions for Python 3.8.

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
... and manage them with dependabot.

I've intentionally left the version in project.toml open and then installed it separately with a file used only on our CI (so as not to put artificial barriers on future users trying to install it somewhere we hadn't anticipated).

I tried using the argument to `build` `--dependency-constraints-txt`. That's unfortunately too new so didn't work.

Setting an environmental variable to `PIP_BUILD_CONSTRAINT` seemed like it might also work, but to me looked a bit more opaque than what I've done here.

For Cython 3.2.x we'll need a slightly more complicated requirements file with different versions for Python 3.8.

comment_data.body:
I'm not set on the path I've used here (.wheel_build_requirements). The main thing is that it should be unique because you can only point dependabot to a directory.

(Although we could just bundle it into the .github directory)

I understand the intention to not break existing build setups, but if you want reproducible builds then `--no-isolation` is really contrary.

It does look like `dependabot` understands `pyproject.toml` by now. Did you try that?
https://github.com/dependabot/dependabot-core/issues/3290
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -19,6 +19,12 @@ test-command = [
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Scanning.py",
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Parsing.py",
 ]
+# For cibuildwheel, use strict version constraints to ensure it's as reproducible as possible.
+# This means not installing from pyproject.toml but instead using a separate, more constrained file.
+# However, keep pyproject.toml build-system.requires open to not artificially constrain users
+# who may be trying to install on future versions of Python with unknown setuptools support. 
+build-frontend = "build; args: --no-isolation"

comment_data.body:
> Did you try that?

I didn't try it (because I don't think there's a good way to test it in advance) but I believe it does.

I was trying to avoid putting a hard version pin in pyproject.toml because that forces the strict version check onto user when they probably don't care.

I agree about the no-isolation though.  That sounds like `PIP_BUILD_CONSTRAINT` might be the better solution here.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -19,6 +19,12 @@ test-command = [
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Scanning.py",
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Parsing.py",
 ]
+# For cibuildwheel, use strict version constraints to ensure it's as reproducible as possible.
+# This means not installing from pyproject.toml but instead using a separate, more constrained file.
+# However, keep pyproject.toml build-system.requires open to not artificially constrain users
+# who may be trying to install on future versions of Python with unknown setuptools support. 
+build-frontend = "build; args: --no-isolation"

comment_data.body:
The current version uses a separate file but doesn't break the isolation in the build process.

That may still be over-complicated, but I'd still prefer not to force a version on future users in pyproject.toml (just because it's always a pain to workaround if it becomes a problem in future).
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -19,6 +19,12 @@ test-command = [
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Scanning.py",
     "cython {project}/Cython/Compiler/Parsing.py",
 ]
+# For cibuildwheel, use strict version constraints to ensure it's as reproducible as possible.
+# This means not installing from pyproject.toml but instead using a separate, more constrained file.
+# However, keep pyproject.toml build-system.requires open to not artificially constrain users
+# who may be trying to install on future versions of Python with unknown setuptools support. 
+build-frontend = "build; args: --no-isolation"

comment_data.body:
> Although we could just bundle it into the .github directory

Yes, we should do that. We already have `cibuildwheel-requirement.txt` in there, we can just put this next to it. Ok, it can be more broadly used via `pyproject.toml`, but in the rare cases when users or distributions build their own Cython wheel, they can probably live with that place.

commit.message:
Pin build requirements versions

... and manage them with dependabot.

I've intentionally left the version in project.toml open and
then installed it separately with a file used only on our CI (so
as not to put artificial barriers on future users trying to install
it somewhere we hadn't anticipated).

I tried using the argument to `build` `--dependency-constraints-txt`.
That's unfortunately too new so didn't work.

Setting an environmental variable to `PIP_BUILD_CONSTRAINT`
seemed like it might also work, but to me looked a bit more
opaque than what I've done here.

For Cython 3.2.x we'll need a slightly more complicated requirements
file with different versions for Python 3.8.

milestone.description:
Main features:
- Pattern matching
- Generics / subscripted container types
- `@cython.collection_type()`
- `restrict`
- more vectorcalls
- more thread safety
- smaller modules, more shared code
- remove Py3.8 support, keep Py3.9+

title: Pin build requirements versions
